title: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
official_title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence
overview: The EU AI Act is the world's first comprehensive horizontal legal framework for artificial intelligence. It establishes a risk-based classification system for AI systems and imposes obligations on providers, deployers, importers, and distributors placing AI systems on the EU market or using AI to serve EU users. The Act also governs general-purpose AI (GPAI) models. Non-compliance carries penalties of up to EUR 35 million or 7% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher.

timeline.milestone: Chapter I (General Provisions) and Chapter II (Prohibited Practices) apply
Chapter III Section 4 (Notified Bodies), Chapter V (GPAI Models), Chapter VII (Governance), Chapter XII (Penalties) apply
Annex I (High-Risk AI in Safety Components) and Annex III (High-Risk AI) obligations fully apply; EU AI database operational
Full application including embedded AI in regulated products (Annex I Part I)
Obligations for high-risk AI systems already in service before 2026 apply

key_definitions.definition: A machine-based system designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy that may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers from the input it receives how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments.
key_definitions.significance: The definition is deliberately broad and covers most modern ML systems. Traditional software with fully deterministic, rule-based logic is generally excluded.

key_definitions.definition: A natural or legal person who develops an AI system or GPAI model or has it developed with a view to placing it on the market or putting it into service under its own name or trademark.
key_definitions.significance: Providers bear the heaviest obligations — conformity assessments, CE marking, registration, and technical documentation for high-risk AI.

key_definitions.definition: A natural or legal person who uses an AI system under its own authority for a professional purpose.
key_definitions.significance: Deployers of high-risk AI systems have substantial obligations: fundamental rights impact assessments, human oversight, transparency to affected persons, and monitoring.

key_definitions.definition: AI systems listed in Annex III — including AI in critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration, justice, and democratic processes — plus AI embedded in regulated products listed in Annex I (machinery, medical devices, vehicles, etc.).
key_definitions.significance: High-risk classification triggers the most onerous obligations. The 2023 NACE classification update expanded the list significantly.

key_definitions.definition: An AI model trained on large amounts of data using self-supervision at scale, that displays significant generality and is capable of performing a range of distinct tasks, and which can be integrated into various downstream systems. Includes large language models and multimodal foundation models.
key_definitions.significance: GPAI providers must produce technical documentation, comply with copyright law, and publish training data summaries. Systemic-risk GPAI (>10^25 FLOPs training compute) face additional adversarial testing and incident reporting requirements.

key_definitions.definition: Risk specific to GPAI models with high-impact capabilities due to their reach, their capabilities, or a combination thereof, including capabilities that could have a significant negative impact on public health, safety, public security, fundamental rights, or society as a whole.
key_definitions.significance: Systemic-risk GPAI providers must perform model evaluations, adversarial testing, report serious incidents to the AI Office, and ensure adequate cybersecurity protection.

prohibited_practices.title: Subliminal manipulation
prohibited_practices.description: AI systems that deploy subliminal techniques beyond a person's consciousness or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques to materially distort behaviour in a way that causes or is likely to cause significant harm.

prohibited_practices.title: Exploitation of vulnerabilities
prohibited_practices.description: AI systems that exploit any of the vulnerabilities of a natural person or a specific group of persons due to their age, disability, or social or economic situation.

prohibited_practices.title: Social scoring by public authorities
prohibited_practices.description: AI systems used for the evaluation or classification of natural persons based on their social behaviour or inferred personal characteristics over a period of time, leading to detrimental or unfavourable treatment of those persons.

prohibited_practices.title: Real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces
prohibited_practices.description: Use of real-time remote biometric identification systems in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement purposes, subject to narrow exceptions.

prohibited_practices.title: Emotion recognition in workplace/education
prohibited_practices.description: AI systems used for emotion recognition in the workplace and educational institutions, except for medical or safety reasons.

prohibited_practices.title: Biometric categorisation inferring sensitive attributes
prohibited_practices.description: AI systems that categorise natural persons based on their biometric data to infer race, political opinions, trade union membership, religious beliefs, sex life, or sexual orientation.

providers_high_risk.title: Quality management system
providers_high_risk.description: Establish, implement, and maintain a quality management system covering the full AI system lifecycle.

providers_high_risk.title: Technical documentation
providers_high_risk.description: Draw up technical documentation demonstrating conformity with all mandatory requirements before placing the system on the market.

providers_high_risk.title: Conformity assessment
providers_high_risk.description: Conduct a conformity assessment procedure before market placement. Many high-risk AI systems require third-party notified body assessment.

providers_high_risk.title: EU database registration
providers_high_risk.description: Register the high-risk AI system in the EU database before placing it on the market or putting it into service.

providers_high_risk.title: CE marking
providers_high_risk.description: Affix CE marking to the AI system before placing it on the EU market.

providers_high_risk.title: Transparency and instructions
providers_high_risk.description: Provide clear instructions for use, including information on capabilities, limitations, and human oversight requirements.

providers_high_risk.title: Logging capabilities
providers_high_risk.description: Ensure AI systems automatically generate logs at the level of detail appropriate to the purpose, retained for at least 6 months.

providers_high_risk.title: Post-market monitoring
providers_high_risk.description: Establish a post-market monitoring plan and report serious incidents to market surveillance authorities within 15 days.

deployers_high_risk.title: Human oversight
deployers_high_risk.description: Ensure that natural persons assigned to human oversight are competent and have the authority to stop or override the AI system.

deployers_high_risk.title: Fundamental rights impact assessment
deployers_high_risk.description: Public-body deployers and private deployers providing public services must conduct a fundamental rights impact assessment before deployment.

deployers_high_risk.title: Monitor in operation
deployers_high_risk.description: Monitor the operation of the AI system, report serious incidents or malfunctions to the provider and national authority.

deployers_high_risk.title: Inform affected persons
deployers_high_risk.description: Inform natural persons who interact with, or are subject to, decisions made by a high-risk AI system that it uses AI.

deployers_high_risk.title: Data governance
deployers_high_risk.description: Ensure that input data used is relevant and sufficiently representative for the system's intended purpose.

gpai_providers.title: Technical documentation
gpai_providers.description: Prepare and maintain technical documentation including training and testing information, compute used, and safety evaluation results.

gpai_providers.title: Copyright compliance
gpai_providers.description: Implement a policy to comply with EU copyright law, including opt-outs from text and data mining.

gpai_providers.title: Training data summary
gpai_providers.description: Publish a sufficiently detailed summary of the training data used.

gpai_providers.title: Systemic-risk model obligations
gpai_providers.description: Perform model evaluations, adversarial testing (red-teaming), report serious incidents to the AI Office, ensure adequate cybersecurity.

high_risk_categories.category: Biometric identification and categorisation of natural persons
Employment, workforce management, self-employment (recruitment, performance evaluation, termination, task allocation)
Essential private and public services (credit scoring, insurance risk, emergency dispatch, benefit eligibility)
Law enforcement (risk assessment of natural persons, lie detection, evidence reliability)
Administration of justice and democratic processes

sme_cap.note: SMEs and startups: lower of the two caps applies

cross_references.relevance: Data protection requirements apply to AI systems processing personal data. Article 22 (automated decisions) interacts directly with high-risk AI deployer obligations.
Amended to cover AI-caused harm; strict liability applies to AI systems in safety-critical contexts.
Proposed directive on fault-based liability for AI damage; pending Council and Parliament adoption.
